title: Accessing the Untapped Brand Leverage Potential: A Strategic Framework from a Capital Market View
abstract: Do firms fully capitalize on the financial strength of their customer-based brand equity (CBBE)? If not, how large is the gap to a benchmark condition where CBBE is financially fully leveraged? Which market strategies, if any, should the firm emphasize to close the gap? This research addresses these questions. The authors introduce a framework and methodology that enables firms to identify and measure their “brand capabilities gap,” which captures the firm’s potential for leveraging CBBE into firm value when the most effective and efficient brand leverage strategy is in place, and, if necessary, take corrective action to realize their brand leverage potential. The authors further examine three market strategy moderators that firms can steer via their marketing programs and tactics to close the brand capabilities gap. The application of the framework and methodology to a wide-ranging sample of firms from 2005 to 2013 reveals that, on average, firms only realize 35% of their financial brand leverage potential. This translates into an average brand capabilities gap worth $2.1 bn per brand, which represents 4.3% of firm value. These findings have important implications for strategic brand analysis and planning and can help firms design a more effective financial brand leverage strategy.

author.Marc Fischer: Faculty of Management, Economics, and Social Sciences, University of Cologne, 50923 Cologne, Germany
author.Simone Wies: Faculty of Economics and Business, Department of Marketing, Goethe University Frankfurt, 60323 Frankfurt am Main, Germany

article.Introduction: Brands are among the most valuable intangible assets of the firm. This value arises from the firm’s previous investments in marketing programs that create specific knowledge about the brand in customers’ minds. To improve marketing productivity, firms regularly monitor the strength of their brands through the eyes of the individual customer and use multidimensional brand ratings such as EquiTrend from Harris Poll, BrandIndex from YouGov, or BAV from Young & Rubicam. These widely used brand ratings follow the concept of customer-based brand equity (CBBE) that has been theoretically developed and defined byKeller (1993, p. 8) as “the differential effect of brand knowledge on consumer response to the marketing of the brand”(italics in the original). Although a significant body of literature has studied how to build and measure CBBE (Aaker 1991,Keller 2013), less attention has been paid to understanding how firms best capture financial value of CBBE (seeFrennea et al. (2019)for a similar observation). The general understanding is that CBBE is a powerful driver of firm value (Srinivasan and Hanssens 2009,Bharadwaj et al. 2011), although this effect can vary enormously. In their meta-analysis,Edeling and Fischer (2016)report a generalized firm-value elasticity with respect to (w.r.t.) CBBE of 0.33 across a range of elasticities from −0.43 to 0.74. The wide variation of brand-firm value effects begs the question of the underlying reasons that have not yet been examined. Why do some firms not financially benefit from an increase in CBBE? Has their CBBE reached its firm-value-maximizing level? Are their brands leveraged in a financially ineffective way? The difference between these two explanations has important implications for the firm’s investment decisions. If CBBE is at its firm-value-maximizing level, no further expansion investment is recommended, all else equal. However, if CBBE is simply leveraged in a financially ineffective way, further investment could help access untapped brand leverage potential. Existing models to measure the CBBE-firm value effect are not designed to inform about the brand’s inherentpotentialto enhance firm value. This calls for a new approach that is transparent about the sources of current brand (in)effectiveness and the brand’s firm value potential from optimal financial brand leverage. We address this research gap and focus on the firm’sfinancial brand leverage strategy, which describes the firm’s approach to converting CBBE into firm value. At the outset of developing such strategy lies recognizing thebrand capabilities gap, which captures the firm’s potential for value creation if the most effective and efficient brand leverage strategy is put in place. Our main contribution is to offer a strategic analysis framework and methodology through which we can identify and measure the brand capabilities gap, and, if necessary, inform corrective action to the firm’s financial brand leverage strategy so that the firm can realize its full brand leverage potential. In developing our framework and methodology, we take a capital-market perspective, assuming that top management places a special emphasis on the expectations of investors. We do not claim that this angle is relevant to all firms and acknowledge that other strategic objectives exist that may override the expectations of the financial markets. However, given that 78% of the S&P 500 firm chief executive officers (CEOs) are compensated in stock (Angelis and Grinstein 2015), we believe that our investor-oriented framework is relevant to a large number of firms. In the next section, we elaborate on the main conceptual and methodological aspects of our contribution to the brand management literature.
article.Contributions to the Brand Management Li: Extended Conceptual CBBE-Firm Value Framework Figure 1introduces the framework that underlies our conceptualization and method (Fischer et al. 2018). The key feature of this framework is the decomposition of how CBBE is converted into firm value. First, in line with our capital market focus, we begin by outlining how firm value is generated. Financial valuation theory posits that firm value arises from the interplay of four financial value drivers (Copeland et al. 2013): thereturn on invested capital(ROIC), theweighted average cost of capital(WACC), theearnings growth rate(EGR), and thesustainability of excess return(SUS). ROIC is a measure of profitability and captures the average rate of return on new investments that the firm expects to generate from its future projects. WACC reflects the capital-structure weighted average of the cost of equity and cost of debt. EGR represents the expected average rate by which earnings grow, whereas this growth only adds value when producing excess return (i.e.,ROIC−WACC> 0). A fundamental assumption of competitive theory is that excess return cannot be maintained perpetually (Demsetz 1982). SUS measures the length of this excess return period and is the capital market equivalent of a competitive advantage. For reading convenience, we use the abbreviated terms and acronyms profitability (ROIC), earnings growth (EGR), capital cost (WACC), and sustainability (SUS) throughout the paper. Table 1.Illustration of Decomposing the CBBE-Firm Value Effect Table 1.Illustration of Decomposing the CBBE-Firm Value Effect Note.Change in firm value is calculated perEquation (2)based on a 10% increase in CBBE; elasticities are taken from the range of estimated values of our empirical application. The relative contribution of each value driver to firm value is not universally fixed and depends on how the capital market evaluates a given firm. Although a value driver’s impact on firm value is exogeneous to brand managers, it is still relevant to brand management as CBBE can activate a more or less relevant value driver. Our framework unfolds a potential disconnect between these two perspectives and decomposes the CBBE-firm value effect into two parts: (i) the relative contribution of each financial value driver to firm value and (ii) the degree to which CBBE activates each value driver. Using this decomposition framework, our suggested methodology enables us to identify the level of firm value the firmcouldachieve had it leveraged its CBBE via the most relevant value driver and in the most efficient way. The difference between this potential firm value and the actual firm value is what we refer to as the firm’sbrand capabilities gap. Second, turning to the brand management focus, we argue, that although the relative contribution of each financial value driver to firm value is exogenous to brand managers, the degree to which CBBE activates each financial value driver is not. We posit that this activation depends on the firm’s market strategy that embraces three basic emphases: brand segmentation, penetration, and expansion. As a further feature of our framework, we therefore introduce the firm’s emphasis on these three market strategies as moderators of how CBBE is leveraged into the value drivers. Consistent with the brand value chain concept byKeller and Lehmann (2003), we refer to these moderators asbrand segmentation emphasis,brand penetration emphasis, andbrand geographic expansion emphasis multiplier. They determine the extent to which CBBE transfers, or “multiplies,” into the value drivers and are a second key component of our framework. Empirical Modeling Framework to Measure the Brand Capabilities Gap Leveraging CBBE in a way that accounts for both the role of strategy emphasis multipliers and the expectations of the capital market requires a new methodology to evaluate the impact of CBBE on firm value. As a further contribution, we develop an empirical modeling framework that integrates all mediation and moderation effects, as illustrated inFigure 1. We apply this method to a broad sample of 598 firms, covering a nine-year period (2005–2013) across a wide range of industries. The calibrated model produces the firm-specific CBBE effect on firm value under actual conditions. More importantly, however, it also enables us to quantify the firm-specific CBBE effect on firm value had the firm activated the most relevant value driver and had it done so in the most efficient way (i.e., reflecting the firm’s ability to leverage CBBE into the four financial value drivers). The difference between the actual and potential firm value produces the brand capabilities gap. Use of Methodology for Strategic Brand Analysis and Planning Building on the brand capabilities gap, we develop a set ofstrategic brand analysis and planningtools that help firms identify and act on critical shortcomings in their CBBE leverage. Specifically, as a final analytical contribution, we develop a normative model that determines the optimal allocation of resources to maximize the impact of CBBE on firm value. This model combines information from the brand capabilities gap analysis with the market strategy emphasis identification to direct CBBE toward the most relevant value driver. Based on how the three strategy emphases help firms convert CBBE into a respective value driver, the model allocates resources by prioritizing the emphasis on the market strategies that are most effective for leveraging CBBE. We obtain optimal allocation outcomes and demonstrate the model’s application value using data from a brand in our sample (Harley Davidson). Existing Literature Our work primarily relates to three streams of the empirical brand management literature. First, it builds on literature that has studied the direct link between CBBE and firm value (Mizik and Jacobson 2008,Bharadwaj et al. 2011). The meta-analysis ofEdeling and Fischer (2016)of this research concludes that, on average, CBBE increases firm value but also highlights that “future studies should put more focus on the heterogeneity in firm value effects” (p. 533), which we follow here. The second stream of studies relevant to our research is the literature that has investigated how CBBE relates to the four financial value drivers (see Table A.1 in Online Appendix A for an overview). Although evidence supports the positive impact of CBBE on profitability (Aaker and Jacobson 2001), insights on the other three value drivers are either sparse (earnings growth and sustainability) or inconclusive (capital cost). Our work builds on these literature streams and seeks to provide explanations for the observed heterogeneity of CBBE-firm value effects. We derive these explanations from the proposed integrative CBBE-firm value framework that examines all four value drivers simultaneously and incorporates the moderating influence of the firm’s market strategy. Third, in doing so, our research adds to a third stream of work that conceptualizes the CBBE impact in the form of a value chain. The model ofKeller and Lehmann (2003)describes how brand investments materialize in shareholder value via customer outcomes and product-market outcomes.Datta et al. (2017)focus on individual dimensions of CBBE and how they translate into product-market outcomes measured by the revenue premium approach. We complement these models by adding a financial-market view and introducing new diagnostic metrics to support strategic brand analysis and planning.
article.Methodology to Measure the Brand Capabil: Decomposing the CBBE-Firm Value Effect We begin with the description of the firm value function that underlies the idea of measuring the effect of CBBE on firm value. Let FV = f(CBBE,Y) denote the firm value function, which is continuous and twice differentiable, and whereYis a column vector summarizing other variables influencing firm value. We do not make any assumptions about the form of f(⋅). For ease of exposition, we add indices for firm and time only in our empirical application, when we also introduce specific functional forms for estimation purposes. Analyzing the total differential of FV, we can restate the firm value function in terms of elasticities (see Online Appendix A for details): Following the framework ofFigure 1, let us now expand the firm value function to incorporate the mediation of CBBE through the four financial value drivers, that is, FV = f[ROIC(CBBE), WACC(CBBE), EGR(CBBE), SUS(CBBE),Y]. Treating again the total differential for this function, we obtain the following expression: Decomposing the CBBE-firm effect into the financial value driver effects offers important insights that are not available from standard models.Table 1illustrates this concept based on three stylized scenarios that differ with regard to capital market relevance (i.e., which value driver is most rewarded) and brand leverage effectiveness (i.e., how CBBE activates each value driver). For ease of exposition, we consider only two value drivers (ROIC and EGR) and assume that the capital market rewards the firm relatively more for improving ROIC than for EGR. In scenario I, the firm is not able to convert its CBBE into any value driver, indicating low brand leverage effectiveness and a low aggregate CBBE-firm value elasticity. In scenario II, the firm is able to convert CBBE into ROIC, which is highly rewarded by the capital market, indicating high brand leverage effectiveness and a high aggregate CBBE-firm value elasticity. For both scenarios, the standard model would produce the correct conclusions regarding CBBE’s impact on firm value. In scenario III, the firm is able to convert CBBE into another value driver, EGR, but the capital market does not reward this value driver as much. As a result, the small aggregate CBBE-firm value elasticity, which standard models would produce, suggests that CBBE has no impact on firm value. However, the decomposition perEquation (2)reveals that the brandcouldactually be financially more effective via ROIC, yet currently activates the “wrong” (i.e., relatively less relevant) financial value driver EGR. The example suggests that it is less important that CBBE drivesanyfinancial value driver but that it activates the mostrelevantvalue drivers to be effectively leveraged to enhance firm value. Measuring the Brand Capabilities Gap To quantify the brand capabilities gap, we introduce a benchmark that accounts for a firm’s ability to influence a particular value driver. To this end, we compare the firm-value effect of CBBE, as perEquation (2), under two conditions: a firm’s CBBE’s actual impact and its potential impact on the value drivers according to its efficient frontier. Benchmarking firms based on their efficient frontier is a well-known concept in the marketing capability literature (Dutta et al. 1999) and helps contextualize a firm’s scope of action. In our application, the efficient frontier is thehighestCBBE effectiveness a firm could achieve given its resources, competitive market conditions, and macroeconomic situation. Raising CBBE effectiveness to its efficient frontier requires activating the most relevant value driver (effectiveness effect) and activating it in the strongest way possible for the respective firm (efficiency effect). However, we do not consider an input-output relation, such as expenditures and sales, but focus on marginal effects that measure the firm’s capability to convert CBBE into financial value drivers. Implicit to this approach is the assumption that the marginal effect is not fixed but can be improved to reach its efficient benchmark. article.Use of the Methodology for Strategic Bra: Our methodology delivers its main value in addressing important questions of strategic brand management that account for a capital market perspective. It stands in the tradition of models developed to produce diagnostic insights without incorporating tactical decision variables such as advertising spend. Several influential models have been developed along these lines within the strategic customer management literature to assess customer value, predict customer activity status, and optimize the balance between customer acquisition and retention investments (Schmittlein et al. 1987,Reinartz et al. 2005,Fader and Hardie 2010).Hanssens et al. (2014)andDatta et al. (2017)have contributed to the product and brand management literature through their models and the accompanying diagnostic metrics for strategic marketing planning. In the following, we demonstrate the value of our methodology in the areas of strategic brand analysis and planning. Strategic brand analysis involves assessing the brand’s strengths and weaknesses, identifying opportunities for improvement, and developing a clear understanding of the brand’s strategic position. Strategic planning, on the other hand, involves using the insights gained from strategic analysis to define strategic emphases and allocate resources to support these emphases.Table 2overviews the various steps of this analytics process in our context of financial brand leverage. Table 3.Operationalization of Key Variables Table 3.Operationalization of Key Variables Note.In their CBBE conceptualization,Harris Poll (2021)label the brand knowledge dimension “Mental availability” and the purchase consideration dimension “Intended behavior.” Strategic Brand Analysis Considering our focus on financial brand leverage, the starting point for strategic brand analysis is the methodology we offer to identify the brand capabilities gap. First, the financial quantification of the brand capabilities gap (Equation (3)) is important to spotlight the economic gains that could be realized from closing the gap. The monetary term in itself, however, is agnostic abouthowto close the gap. As a second step in the analysis, firms hence need to understand which value drivers are most instrumental to closing the gap. The priority matrix inFigure 2offers a simple but powerful aid for this analysis. Based on the elasticity terms for each value driver (FVD), the matrix compares the relative importance of a value driver for firm value (xaxis; based on εFV,FVD) with how efficiently CBBE is translated into that value driver (yaxis; based on εFVD,CBBE). Beginning withFigure 2(a), if a value driver is particularly relevant for firm value but CBBE does not activate it, it is an inefficient CBBE lever (Missed Opportunity). If the firm is strong in leveraging CBBE for a relatively unimportant value driver, the driver is an ineffective brand lever (False Opportunity). The financial benefits are highest if the firm is strong in leveraging CBBE into the most important value drivers (Sweet Spot). By positioning each value driver in its respective cell, firms can easily assess which value drivers require attention and should be prioritized for maximum CBBE impact on firm value (seeFigure 2(b)for an example of how Harley Davidson’s value drivers are categorized based on our empirical findings). Strategic Brand Planning: Three Strategy Emphasis Multipliers of Financial Brand Leverage Having prioritized the financial value drivers, we next examine under which type of market strategy firms can best leverage CBBE into a certain value driver. The basic strategy framework posits three (proto)typical foci in a firm’s market strategy: market segmentation, penetration, and expansion. The emphasis on the type of market strategy is a fundamental decision about the preferred mode through which the firm wants to grow its business and consequently how it employs and builds its brand along this strategy. We define abrand segmentation emphasisas a strategic focus on brand profiling and segmentation activities that serve distinct segments of consumers. Abrand penetration emphasisdescribes a strategic focus on activities that increase the purchase consideration of the brand given the level of brand knowledge built in customer memory. The stronger the purchase consideration, the deeper the consumers’ purchase relationship with the brand. Abrand geographic expansion emphasisinvolves a strategic focus on activities that strengthen the brand more widely and in a more balanced manner across geographic regions so that the brand becomes the preferred choice not only in local markets but nationwide. Although firms might consider and invest in all three types of strategies in their brand management, realistically, they have to make tradeoffs about which strategies to prioritize at a given time, which is why we consider them strategyemphasesin brand management. We conceptualize the strategy emphases as moderators, or multipliers, respectively, for the marginal CBBE effect on the four value drivers. The concept of multipliers goes back toKeller and Lehmann (2003)and their brand value chain, in which they refer to program and customer multipliers as linking factors that determine how brand value created at one stage (e.g., CBBE) transfers to the next stage (e.g., shareholder value).Table 3shows how we measure the three strategy emphasis multipliers; in doing so, we follow the tradition of strategy researchers (Fang et al. 2011) of using objective, secondary data that reflect outcomes connected with a strategy not directly observed. Specifically, we exploit the raw consumer-level data underlying the aggregate CBBE score to capture the three strategies. Beyond empirical appeal, this approach is also attractive from a managerial perspective because the multipliers are based on data regularly collected to measure CBBE, can be influenced through marketing and communication tactics, and often relate to KPIs (e.g., funnel conversion) that enjoy considerable managerial attention. Moreover, they exploit consumer information along all three dimensions of consumer-brand relationships: across consumers (segmentation emphasis), within consumers (penetration emphasis), and across geographic markets (geographic expansion emphasis), and thereby leverage important information not available through the aggregate CBBE rating. While we assume that all three multipliers can moderate how CBBE translates into the four value drivers, we acknowledge that they might do so at different degrees. Because the theory behind the multipliers is not sufficiently developed, we refrain from developing formal hypotheses about their effects. Instead, we discuss the financial value drivers that we expect to be predominantly activated by the respective multiplier. Segmentation Emphasis Multiplier. A brand may be positioned in a way that appeals to more or less distinctive segments of consumers. In operational terms, a more pronounced segmentation emphasis is hence likely to result in a wider range of how consumers assess a brand, indicating more clustering toward extreme evaluations that reflect defined differences between segments. As such, brand segmentation is an across-consumer multiplier that is derived from how CBBE scores are distributed across consumers. Examples of brands emphasizing strong segmentation in our sample are Apple and Starbucks, characterized by a large variation in how consumers rate the brands. Expedia and Macy’s rate lower on brand segmentation with a more homogeneous CBBE evaluation across consumers. Because serving a concise group of loyal customers holds important price and cost advantages, we expect that a segmentation emphasis dominantly strengthens the effect of CBBE on ROIC. This notion is supported by a long research tradition that has established the benefits of a well-defined and loyal customer base for charging higher prices (Albert and Merunka 2013), reducing acquisition costs (Stahl et al. 2012), and lowering other marketing and sales costs to prevent defections to rival offers (Aaker 1991) — all improvements to the efficiency of brand-related investments, which we expect to strengthen the link between CBBE and profitability. Penetration Emphasis Multiplier. Brand penetration aims at increasing the purchase consideration of the brand given the level of brand knowledge held in consumer memories, by for instance enhancing the use share or attracting nonusers of the brand (Farris et al. 2010). From this logic, a brand penetration emphasis is a within-consumer multiplier that resembles the customer conversion ratio in typical brand funnel analysis (Fischer et al. 2011b;Keller 2013, p. 80f). According toKeller (1993, p. 2), CBBE develops “when the consumer is familiar with the brand and holds some favorable, strong, and unique brand associations in memory,” which conforms to the brand knowledge component. Purchase consideration complements his concept and refers to the consumer response to marketing of the brand. The penetration emphasis multiplier measures the difference between the two components, as this difference mirrors the power of the brand to convert customers’ brand knowledge into wider and deeper purchase relationships with the brand. Examples of brands with a lower penetration emphasis level are Citibank and Dell, which are characterized by relatively low purchase intent given consumers’ overall knowledge of the brand. In contrast, Tiffany and Under Armour rate higher on penetration emphasis in our sample. We expect that a penetration emphasis primarily improves the link of CBBE to SUS, as prior literature has emphasized the strong “staying power” of brands with high personal relevance and consideration, which is the key prerequisite for purchase intention, in comparison with brand knowledge (Mizik and Jacobson 2008, p. 16).Aaker (2011, p. 18) highlights that a strategy focused on personal relevance and consideration generates advantages that “will be projected to last” and serves as one of the strongest routes to long-term growth. Examining the interplay between personal brand relevance and consideration (purchase consideration) and brand knowledge,Mizik and Jacobson (2008)note that, although both facets have to be considered jointly, an emphasis on personal relevance stands out in increasing value through a sustainable competitive advantage and generating carryover effects to future periods. We therefore expect a penetration emphasis to dominantly strengthen the effect of CBBE on SUS. Geographic Expansion Emphasis Multiplier. Brand geographic expansion describes how the strength of the brand is distributed across geographic markets,1that is, whether those consumers who prefer the brand are concentrated in only a few states or more evenly spread across many states. The stronger the expansion emphasis, the less geographically clustered the consumers who prefer the brand. As such, brand geographic expansion emphasis is an across-market multiplier that aggregates market-level CBBE scores in an entropy-based metric. Our conceptualization followsFang et al. (2011)who use an entropy-based approach to describe customer asset breadth and argue that broad assets provide diverse,expansivecontexts for extracting value from the unique offerings that the brand embodies. Examples of brands with geographically expanded strength in our sample are CBS and Coca-Cola. In contrast, brand strength is geographically more concentrated for Exxon. A more broadly distributed brand strength offers substantial growth opportunities for the brand, which is why we expect that expansion emphasis strengthens particularly the effect of CBBE on EGR. A broadened geographic brand strength suggests that consumer preferences are not limited to local preferences and hence offers more opportunities to scale up at a national level, possibly with varying positioning (Bronnenberg et al. 2007). A broader distribution of strength is also linked to stronger (nationwide) retail support for the brand, which is a crucial gatekeeper for growth activities, including extending product lines and expanding into related or new product categories. Retailers are known to use multimarket leadership in assessing sales volume, competitive clout, and traffic-generating appeal (Völckner and Sattler 2006) and thereby support how firms can employ CBBE to grow the business. Strategic Brand Planning: Resource Allocation to Support Strategy Emphases The final stage of the analytics process involves planning the allocation of resources to support and implement the three market strategies according to their relevance for leveraging CBBE into important value drivers. To achieve this goal, we develop an analytic model to allocate resources across activities that drive the strategy emphasis multipliers to their optimal level, that is, the level that maximizes firm value from brand leverage. This normative resource allocation model follows the logic of previous allocation solutions that have been developed for marketing resource allocation problems in other contexts (Fischer et al. 2011a). Problem Setup. Consider the generalized firm value function underlyingEquation (2). Let us expand this function by describing themarginal effectof CBBE on ROIC, WACC, EGR, and SUS as a continuous, twice differentiable function g(⋅) of the three emphasis multipliers, which we summarize in vectorzfor ease of exposition:∂𝐅⁢𝐕⁢𝐃/∂⁢𝐂𝐁𝐁𝐄=𝐠⁡(𝐳)∂F⁢V⁢D/∂⁢CBBE=g⁡(z), whereFVDis the vector of financial value drivers. We do not make any further assumptions about the form of these relations. In our subsequent empirical application, however, we specify specific functional forms to estimate the effects of interest. Let C(z) denote a linear-additive cost function that adds up the financial resources invested into the respective market strategy. The investments increase with the level of the strategy emphasis multipliers. We do not make any assumption about therateof increase; that is, it can be linear, decreasing, or increasing. Thus, the marginal investment cost may change with the multiplier level and vary across multipliers. Investment costs include expenditures directly spent on a marketing program to implement the market strategy, for example, a campaign targeting a loyal customer segment to cultivate the distinct brand profile, as well as resources that are valued using internal cost rates. These resources include management time for developing and executing operational marketing and sales plans to implement the strategy. Finally, we assume that the firm has set a fixed investment budget, denoted withB, as resources are usually limited. We acknowledge that this budget will be limited by financial resources available which, in turn, could be influenced by the strength of the brand. Nevertheless, we consider a static budget allocation problem, instead of a more complex dynamic overall budget setting problem, for two reasons. First, evidence suggests that budget setting and resource allocation are usually dealt with separately in practice. Second, the flat maximum principle shows that the (expected) gains from better allocation far outweigh the gains from finding the optimal overall budget (Fischer et al. 2011a). Recall that our premise is that investments in activities to improve the strategy emphasis multipliers are always associated with an improvement of CBBE, not the other way around. Thus, we consider the situation where firm value changes due to a positive change in CBBE, that is,dCBBE> 0. The firm objective therefore is to maximize the value that accrues from the change in CBBE2 Solving the Lagrange equations leads to the following general solution (see Online Appendix A): General Insights. The optimization is static in such a way that we do not model the optimal dynamic path of achieving a desired multiplier level, which is left for future research. However, the optimal solution already offers an array of important general insights. First, the resources that should be assigned to a strategy emphasis multiplier increase proportional to its contribution to firm value relative to the other multipliers. The allocation weightvlcaptures this contribution and corresponds to the total firm value effect due to an increase in emphasis multiplierl. The total firm value effect of a multiplier is the sum of its partial effects mediated via the financial value drivers. Second, ceteris paribus, the solution suggests that more resources should be allocated to those multipliers that are able to activate the most relevant value driver as reflected in its firm-value elasticityεFV,FVD. A financial value driver is hardly under the control of brand management. But management can enhance the CBBE impact on the focal value driver by shifting more resources to activities focused at the multipliers that demonstrate a greater capacity to strengthen the related CBBE impact per𝜀(∂𝐹𝑉𝐷/∂⁢𝐶⁢𝐵𝐵𝐸),𝑧𝑙ε(∂FVD/∂⁢C⁢BBE),zl. article.Empirical Model Specification: Our research design follows the suggested framework ofFigure 1and centers around the four model equations for the financial value drivers: profitability, capital cost, earnings growth, and sustainability. We overview modeling requirements first, followed by the model specifications, and present a visual of the various steps in our empirical analysis in the Online Appendix, Figure A.1. Modeling Requirements of Financial Value Driver Equations Expectations. Firm valuation is based on investors’ expectations about the stream of future cash flows, which is why the financial value driver variables representexpectationsabout profitability, capital cost, earnings growth, and sustainability. When modeling how these expectations are formed, we follow the literature in accounting and finance that assumes relationships to be linear in variables and parameters (Roychowdhury 2006). We retrieve expected earnings growth from an established large-scale analyst forecast sample. Given that survey information is not available for the other variables, we estimate expected values of these financial value drivers, as detailed below. Heterogeneity. We need to control for idiosyncratic differences that arise from pooling data across firms and industries. To do so, we include firm size and market concentration as observed heterogeneity variables. In addition, we adopt a random effects approach that assumes a normal distribution of the firm-specific intercepts (Fischer and Himme 2017), allowing us to measure firm-specific effects while retaining cross-sectional variation in the panel data, which would be lost by including firm dummies. We verify that the random effects do not confound the estimation of our focal effects (see Online Appendix D and Table D.3). In line with our research focus, we also assume the parameters for CBBE to be firm-specific, modeled similarly to the intercepts. Hierarchy. Our premise that the three strategy emphasis multipliers moderate the marginal effect of CBBE on the financial value drivers introduces a hierarchy to the model. Accounting for this hierarchy, we allow the firm-specific conditional mean of the CBBE parameter to shift by the respective multiplier. Dynamics. We include the lagged dependent variable to account for carryover effects and for different initial conditions (Tuli and Bharadwaj 2009). Another advantage is that the impact of other predictors can be interpreted more readily as (Granger) causal. To alleviate the concern that the carryover coefficient might cause identification problems by also picking up firm heterogeneity, we instrument the lagged values with their deviations from the firm-specific mean to isolate the true carryover effect, that is, the lagged variable is demeaned of its firm-specific mean (Fischer and Himme 2017). We also check that the time series of the four financial value drivers and CBBE are stationary. Based on the Fisher-type augmented Dickey-Fuller panel unit-root test (Choi 2001), we cannot reject the assumption of stationary time series. Another important identifying assumption is that the error terms are not serially correlated, which we also test for (see section on identification). Control Variables. Derived from a systematic literature review of prior research in finance, accounting, marketing, and strategy, we include various control variables that have been shown to influence the financial value drivers. Firm-specific controls cover marketing and strategic variables (e.g., expenditures on advertising and research and development (R&D)), financial variables (e.g., financial leverage), and variables of operational efficiency (e.g., operating margin). We also account for economy-wide, period-specific influences by incorporating the growth in U.S. gross domestic product. article.Data and Estimation: Data Sources and Sample We collected data on an annual basis from various databases, including Harris Poll for CBBE data, COMPUSTAT, Bloomberg, the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP), and I/B/E/S for data on financial value drivers and control variables. Our data collection covers the period 2005–2013. The Harris Poll database is our sampling frame and includes 13,491,943 CBBE respondent-brand-year observations in total. From this database, we obtain 598 publicly listed U.S. companies, covering major industry sectors, such as telecommunication services, consumer packaged goods, and consumer durables. The total number of firm-year observations is 5,382. We acknowledge that the sampling frame is biased toward larger brands owned by larger firms, which tend to offer more complete financial data, a limitation we share with prior studies using similar brand data sources (Bharadwaj et al. 2011). Because we do not observe all variables (>30) for each firm-year, the effective sample size is considerably smaller. To minimize potential survivorship bias and increase the statistical power of our analyses, we do not impose restrictions on firms being included in the samples, and as a result, sample size varies by equation. However, our smallest estimation sample does not differ significantly in terms of sales, liquidity, leverage, operating margin, and industry coverage from the full sample of firms for which we have CBBE data (Online Appendix. Table B.1). We also carefully checked whether our sample is subject to a potential selection or measurement bias due to missing values for the advertising and R&D variables but do not find evidence for such biases (see Online Appendix B for a detailed discussion). Measures CBBE. We use the Harris Poll EquiTrend score, indexed from 0 to 100, to measure CBBE (Harris Poll 2021, table 3). Following prior practice (Rego et al. 2009,Bharadwaj et al. 2011), we aggregate mean ratings of different brands for multibrand firms, though the majority in our sample are monobrand firms (>80%) that own one dominant brand that accounts for all or the lion’s share of the business. We adjust the firm’s CBBE rating relative to its mean industry score to make it comparable across industries with different competitive environments and help reduce endogeneity issues (Frennea et al. 2019). Strategy Emphasis Multipliers. We measuresegmentation emphasisby the dispersion of CBBE ratings for each brand across all respondents. To control for different levels of the mean (MinCBBE= 20.95, MaxCBBE= 78.64), we compute the coefficient of variation, which is a scaled version of the standard deviation.Penetration emphasisis the average difference between the respondent’s purchase consideration rating and their brand knowledge rating (labeled mental availability by the data provider and calculated as the mean of their familiarity and quality ratings) of the brand. To measuregeographic expansion emphasis, we compute how the strength of a brand varies across the 50 U.S. states using the standard entropy measure (seeAppendix Bfor details). A value of zero means that brand strength is concentrated in only one state; higher values imply that brand strength is more evenly distributed across more states in the country. We refer to Online Appendix B where we discuss the logic behind our multipliers’ measurement approach in more detail and provide evidence on the convergent and nomological validity of the three metrics. Financial Value Drivers. Firm value (FV) is the sum of the average market value of equity and book value of debt and operationalized as inKoller et al. (2020). We compute profitability as net operating profit after tax over invested capital. Our measure of cost of capital is the firm’s cost of capital as retrieved from Bloomberg. Earnings growth is the five-year consensus forecast of analysts provided by I/B/E/S. Sustainability is a latent construct and not observable. Assuming efficient capital markets, however, it is implicitly incorporated in a firm’s current valuation (seeAppendix B). We refer toTable 3for more details on the operationalization of the focal variables. Control Variables. For the sake of brevity, we discuss the operationalization of control variables in the Online Appendix, Table B.4. This appendix also provides details on data sources, such as the COMPUSTAT data categories we use for computing our variables.Table 4presents the descriptive statistics for all variables in our data, and the Online Appendix, Table B.5, provides corresponding correlations. Model Estimation and Identification Financial Value Driver Models. We use a simulated maximum likelihood approach for estimating the heterogenous model parameters ofEquations (6)–(9) (Greene 2012). This estimator produces conditional means of firm-specific parameters that are comparable to posterior means estimated in Bayesian analysis. The large variation in our focal variables across and within firms provides the source for identifying effects. A major concern that threatens the validity of these effects, as common with observational data, is that our focal variable (CBBE) could be correlated with the error term and bias our estimates.Germann et al. (2015)review at full length the types of endogeneity that are common to models of firm performance as ours and offer remedies. In the following, we provide a summary of the issues relevant to our models and relegate their comprehensive discussion to Online Appendix C. Omitted Variables of Financial and Operational Performance. Firm performance as reflected in the financial value drivers is not only the result of CBBE but can also be driven by factors of operational performance and financial conditions, which in turn might also facilitate investments that lead to higher CBBE. We control for these factors by carefully selecting relevant controls of operational performance and financial conditions as reported in extant finance and accounting literature to reduce this source of endogeneity (see Online Appendix A, Table A.2). Omitted Marketing and Nonfinancial Variables. Prior research shows that marketing investments, such as advertising, R&D, and selling expenditures, that are instrumental to build CBBE also directly impact the financial performance of firms (Edeling and Fischer 2016). In addition, both the accounting and marketing performance literature stresses the importance of nonfinancial metrics for explaining the residual variance in traditional firm performance models that rely on “hard” financial metrics. We include marketing-related expenditures as controls and check for a potentially biasing influence of other nonfinancial metrics (i.e., customer satisfaction, customer asset strength, and corporate reputation), but do not find evidence for a bias (see the Online Appendix, Table C.1). Simultaneity. To control for potential (contemporaneous) simultaneity effects, we include lagged values of the predictor variables in the equations. It is also possible that firms anticipate investors’ expectations for financial value drivers, which in turn influences current and prior firm investments in CBBE to meet these expectations. To minimize this type of simultaneity, we follow the recent marketing performance literature (McAlister et al. 2016,Frennea et al. 2019) and measure CBBE relative to the industry average instead of using absolute CBBE levels because relative CBBE levels cannot be realistically set in anticipation of performance outcomes. Unobserved Time-Invariant Effects on Performance. Unobserved firm-specific effects could present another source of variable omission bias. For example, firms with strong brands have an advantage in attracting more competent talent that could result in superior firm performance (Tavassoli et al. 2014). To capture these and other unobserved time-invariant firm-specific effects, we exploit the panel data structure and estimate firm-specific effects as described earlier. Unobserved Time-Varying Effects on Performance. There could also be unobserved time-varying effects, for example, a major restructuring effort to adopt to a changing market environment, that impact both firm performance and CBBE and introduce a correlation between CBBE and the error term. Such influence should manifest in serially correlated firm performance measures, which we address by including the lagged dependent variable (and other financial controls as mentioned before). Carryover and Cumulative Evolvement of Effects. Advertising campaigns, earned publicity in social media, new product campaigns, and other brand-related activities happen during the year. Their impact on the financial value drivers is not necessarily immediate but unfolds over time with a carryover from one year to the next. By including the lagged dependent variable, we control for such serially correlated firm performance. This specification is consistent with a continuous geometric carryover process as in the partial adjustment model (Hanssens et al. 2001, p. 147f). The identifying assumption here is that there is no serial correlation present in the error term. The autocorrelation coefficients inEquations (6)–(9) are close to zero: −0.020 (ROIC), −0.053 (EGR), −0.026 (WACC), and −0.006 (SUS). The Durbin-Watson statistic and the WD test for dynamic panel data (Born und Breitung 2016) do not suggest that the errors are serially correlated. Efficient Frontier Models. Equation (10)is estimated using simulated maximum likelihood (Greene 2012) with identification coming from the large variation across firms and time. To address the simultaneity situation arising from the reciprocal inclusion of other elasticities, we use a control function, which is especially suited to deal with the nonstandard frontier error specification (Papies et al. 2017). Profitability, earnings growth, and capital cost serve as outside instruments in their respective elasticity equations, and the industry average of the respective elasticity is added as a Hausman-style instrument. These instruments are strong with incrementalFvalues > 10. Although we address simultaneity when estimating the models, we note that the main objective of calibratingEquation (10)is the prediction of the efficient frontier, for which potential endogeneity issues play a less prominent role.

The estimated means and standard deviations for the firm-specific CBBE parameters are significant in all four equations, pointing to substantial variation in the brand-leverage effects across firms and corroborating our research motivation to study the differential effects CBBE can have on financial value drivers and firm value. Significant coefficients of the control variables show the expected direction. CBBE Parameter Estimates. We first consider the models without the strategy emphasis multipliers. CBBE has the expected positive influence on profitability (̅𝑎2=0.119a¯2=0.119;p< 0.01) and on earnings growth (̅𝑏2=0.035b¯2=0.035;p< 0.05). The average impact of CBBE on capital cost is also positive, suggesting that higher CBBE drives up capital cost (̅𝑐2=0.015c¯2=0.015;p <0.01). Finally, we find strong evidence for the role of CBBE in establishing a competitive advantage that ensures a longer period of excess return for the firm, as visible in the estimated parameter on sustainability (̅𝑑1=0.565d¯1=0.565;p< 0.01). Strategy Emphasis Parameter Estimates. When including the strategy emphasis multipliers to the model, the interpretation of the constant of the parameter function changes. It no longer measures the average CBBE effect on the focal value driver across firms but represents a scalar that is added to the multiplier effects to adjust the location of the parameter distribution. The average CBBE effect of the distribution of individual parameters (not reported) is still positive and significant for all value drivers. The estimation results reveal a distinct pattern of how the strategy emphasis influences the effect of CBBE on the value drivers. Notably, we do not find evidence for any multiplier to drive the impact of CBBE on capital cost. Segmentation emphasis turns out to be relevant for driving profitability (𝛾𝑎,2=0.225γa,2=0.225;p< 0.01), but not for any of the other value drivers. Geographic expansion emphasis strengthens the effect of CBBE on profitability (𝛾𝑎,3=0.015γa,3=0.015;p< 0.01) and earnings growth (𝛾𝑏,3=0.011γb,3=0.011;p< 0.01). The impact of CBBE on earnings growth is also contingent on a penetration emphasis (𝛾𝑏,1=0.070γb,1=0.070;p< 0.01), which is also the only significant contingent factor for sustainability (𝛾𝑑,1=0.772γd,1=0.772;p< 0.01). Given the estimated parameters cannot be directly compared across financial value drivers measured at different scales, we present standardized effects at the bottom ofTables 5and6. A distinct pattern becomes apparent: brand segmentation emphasis is an impactful moderator for profitability, whereas penetration emphasis is the most influential moderator for sustainability. Finally, geographic expansion emphasis has a strong moderating effect on earnings growth and profitability. Thus, the strategy emphasis multipliers indeed differ in their relevance for financial value drivers, which has important implications for designing an optimal financial brand leverage strategy. Robustness Checks We performed several robustness checks to assess the validity of our results. Specifically, we confirm that our findings are robust to alternative operationalizations of the strategy emphasis multipliers. We also considered the possibility that the three items that form the CBBE construct may directly impact the value drivers or are partially or fully mediated via CBBE.3Specification tests that compared these alternative models with our focal model, which assumes full mediation and moderation via the multipliers, predominantly preferred our focal model. We report on these and additional robustness checks in Online Appendix D. The additional tests address the appropriateness of specifying random firm-specific effects and the stability of results with respect to annual fixed effects, for which we report no qualitative differences to our focal results. We also provide details on a simultaneous equations system with correlated errors, a subsample of firms with a U.S. revenue share of >50%, and the use of U.S. CBBE as a proxy for a latent global rating. These robustness checks support our results. Capital Market Test of Financial Brand Leverage Strategy We also tested our key premise that investors reward firms for the effectiveness of their brand leverage strategy. To this end, we constructed a variable that measures how close the fit between optimal emphasis multipliers, as implied by the first-order conditions to solveEquation (4), and their observed values is. We then added this variable to a standard stock-market model that explains abnormal returns (Mizik and Jacobson 2008). For the sake of brevity, we report full details of this analysis in Online Appendix D. The results provide external evidence for the extended CBBE-firm value framework with its normative implications for resource allocation to support the emphasized market strategies. Efficient Frontier Models Table 7presents the estimation results for the efficient frontier models. Note that the cost-of-capital equation represents a cost frontier, so that parameters need to be interpreted in the opposite direction. Higher CBBE drives up the efficient frontier as expected. More firm resources (cash, credit, and customer-devoted) tend to expand the frontier. We also observe stronger efficient CBBE elasticities for services and in industries with predominantly B2C customers. However, the frontier cannot be expanded for all CBBE elasticities at the same time; the cross-effects of other elasticities are mostly substitutive. To begin with actual CBBE elasticities, we find that CBBE has the strongest impact on profitability (−𝜀𝑅𝑂𝐼𝐶,𝐶𝐵𝐵𝐸=0.43ε−ROIC,CBBE=0.43), followed by sustainability (−𝜀𝑆𝑈𝑆,𝐶𝐵𝐵𝐸=0.38ε−SUS,CBBE=0.38) and earnings growth (−𝜀𝐸𝐺𝑅,𝐶𝐵𝐵𝐸ε−EGR,CBBE= 0.14). Specifically, the average efficient elasticities are 0.65 (profitability), 0.63 (sustainability), 0.42 (earnings growth), and 0.03 (cost of capital). Table 8.Summary of Elasticity Estimates Table 8.Summary of Elasticity Estimates Note.The number of observations varies depending on the availability of information for computation. aElasticities for earnings growth and sustainability are identical by construction (seeAppendix A). We also find considerable variation across firms in terms of firm value elasticities with respect to financial value drivers. This finding confirms that investors differ in their assessment of the relative importance that a specific value driver has for a given firm. On average, we find that capital cost exerts the strongest impact on firm value (−1.35), followed by earnings growth and sustainability (both 0.41), and eventually profitability (0.33). The resulting actual CBBE-firm value elasticity takes on an average value of 0.11. As expected, the elasticity varies widely across firms as is reflected in the large standard deviation of 0.68. Notably, 34% of CBBE-firm value elasticities are negative. The mean efficient CBBE-firm value elasticity amounts to a significantly higher value of 0.54, pointing to a significant brand capabilities gap. Table E.1 in Online Appendix E offers further details on this gap, assuming a 10% increase in CBBE. According to this analysis, the average firm would have increased its firm value by $1.15 bn underactualconditions. Assuming the firm closes the gap to its efficient frontier, this value gain increases to $3.29 bn. Hence, we identify an impressive mean brand capabilities gap of $2.14 bn that corresponds to an increase in firm value of 4.3%. On average, firms have utilized only 35% of their brand leverage capabilities, albeit the degree of utilization differs strongly across industries. As highlighted before, the large brand leverage potential can reflect two effects: the brand has not activated the most relevant value driver (effectiveness effect); and actual CBBE elasticities w.r.t. the value drivers are below their efficient frontier levels (efficiency effect).
article.Managerial Application: Our framework and the derived analytics process (Table 3) offer clear guidelines for brand strategy executives to close the brand capabilities gap. We next demonstrate the usability of this framework through the example of Harley Davidson, an iconic American brand covered in our sample. Beginning with the strategic analysis, we identify a surprisingly low CBBE-firm value elasticity of 0.062 (perEquation (2)). In contrast, the benchmark CBBE-firm value elasticity from an efficient frontier amounts to 0.471, pointing to a significant unused brand potential that, in economic terms, translates into a brand capabilities gap of $656 million (assuming a 10% increase in CBBE; perEquation (3)). We use the estimated individual elasticities and apply the priority matrix ofFigure 2to understand the reason for this gap and to evaluate the strategic importance of each financial value driver. We exclude WACC from this analysis given our prior estimation results do not provide clear guidance on how this driver can be systematically activated through brand management activities.Figure 2(b)visualizes that ROIC is an ineffective brand lever: the brand is strong in driving ROIC, but it does not benefit much from this strength given the low relevance of ROIC for firm value creation. EGR and SUS, on the other hand, are inefficient brand levers: both value drivers are relatively more important but Harley Davidson is not very efficient in activating them, missing an opportunity to unlock untapped potential for value creation. It becomes evident that the firm should prioritize EGR and SUS to exploit its brand leverage potential and thus focus on activities that strengthen the link between CBBE and both EGR and SUS. Moving to strategic planning, and bearing in mind the prioritization of EGR and SUS, brand penetration and geographic expansion turn out as effective strategy emphases that could help accomplish such activation (see the standardized multiplier coefficients inTables 5and6). Apparently, Harley Davidson pursued a very successful segmentation strategy as reflected in a high level of its segmentation emphasis multiplier (0.52), which puts the brand in the top 20% group of brands in our sample (MedianSEG= 0.37). The level of its penetration emphasis multiplier is considerably lower (−0.79), and belongs to the bottom 10% of brands (MedianPEN= −0.12). The expansion emphasis multiplier level (3.76) ranges in the middle field of brands (MedianEXP= 3.68), still offering room for improvement. We conclude that Harley Davidson should reconsider its strong emphasis on brand segmentation and instead strengthen brand penetration and geographic expansion to benefit more from the strength of its brand. However, how many resources should the company reallocate to support such strategy shift? The suggested allocation model (seeEquation (5)) helps provide an answer to this question. BecauseEquation (5)can only be solved numerically and firms are reluctant to accept black box recommendations, we apply a heuristic that does not require numerical optimization but only uses the available elasticity estimates. For lack of space, we comment on the heuristic in detail in Online Appendix F.Table 9shows the outcome of the allocation task. Based on our resource allocation heuristic, Harley Davidson should use 65% of its brand-investment resources to emphasize brand penetration among target customers, while 33% of its resources should be used to expand the brand’s strength across geographic regions. For the moment, there is no need to further expand investments in brand segmentation. For further details, see Online Appendix F. bTo calculate the heuristic allocation weight for PEN, as an example, proceed as follows. Multiply the elasticity values of the first three rows by each other for column EGR and SUS and divide the sum of the products by the cost divisor for PEN: (0.367 × 0.352 × 2.480 + 0.367 × 0.336 × 1.470)/1 = 0.502. It is important to note, however, that the proposed reallocation of resources is not a permanent recommendation. To the extent that Harley Davidson improves leveraging its CBBE for enhancing EGR and SUS, the allocation rule automatically reallocates resources to reemphasize segmentation to strengthen CBBE’s impact on ROIC. It also does not imply that the brand should abandon investments in its distinct profile and heritage that attracted loyal customers in the past, because this could result in declining ROIC. Lower ROIC, in turn, will only raise the importance of this value driver over the importance of EGR and SUS and signal that brand leverage should be improved w.r.t. to ROIC. These contextual considerations show that strategic brand analysis and planning is a constant process, and firms are well advised to regularly review this process.
article.Discussion: Previous literature has stressed the wide differences in CBBE-firm value effects across firms and called for research to understand the reasons for this heterogeneity (Edeling and Fischer 2016). In this article, we address this question. We introduce a decomposition of the CBBE-firm value effect through the four financial value drivers and develop a measure that quantifies the potential for increasing firm value: the brand capabilities gap. Our empirical analysis suggests that this gap is substantial for many brands and that further strategic brand analysis and planning is warranted to close this gap. We therefore introduce three strategy emphasis multipliers that help strengthen the link between CBBE and value drivers in distinct ways. We also develop a normative resource allocation model from which we can derive straightforward practical implications which enable firms to better align their financial brand leverage strategy with the objective of firm value maximization. In the following, we discuss the implications of our work and position it in the larger context of strategic brand management. What Are the Implications for Strategic Brand Management Research? Examining the firm’s financial brand leverage strategy extends existing strategic brand management literature in important ways. First, it highlights the neglected connection between how brands create value for customers (i.e., through generating CBBE) and for the firm (i.e., through leveraging CBBE), and presents a framework that links the two. Despite the brand being a critical component of corporate strategy and investment decisions, prior research has not offered a systematic examination and modeling framework to appropriate the financial value of a brand (Frennea et al. 2019). Second, our framework suggests that the capital market might play a role in brand management when the firm modifies its strategic brand planning in response to the capital market’s preferences for a specific value driver. This is a novel contribution to the brand management literature, which has mostly focused on product-market drivers of brand management. Third, our research adds theoretical depth by studying three strategy emphasis multipliers that distinctively influence how CBBE activates the financial value drivers and thereby offer new perspectives to enhance brand management competencies. What Are the Implications for Strategic Brand Management Practice? Our framework and findings on the role of the strategy emphasis multipliers also entail important practical insights, including resource allocation recommendations (seeTable 2for the steps in this process). This is an important contribution to strategic brand management, given that brand strategy decisions often lack a quantitative base in absence of data, models, and tailored empirical evidence. It is also worthwhile to note that our framework is not only relevant for public firms but for any business owner that seeks to increase the value of their firm. Although stock market investors are often perceived to be overly short-term oriented (Mizik and Jacobson 2007), the corporate valuation model we rely on is not swayed by such myopic considerations; the value driver elasticities do not change in short intervals but are rather sticky as reflected in their high autocorrelation ranging from 0.60 to 0.78. Instead, the valuation model encourages a balanced and long-term approach to strategic decision making, which is equally relevant for privately owned firms. Likewise, the three market strategies of brand segmentation, penetration, and expansion are fundamental to any business, and executives would like to know which of these strategies to best emphasize in leveraging their CBBE. Even without access to information on value driver and CBBE elasticities, it is possible to use our framework and follow a simple strategy guidance schema. Ignoring WACC for known reasons, we only need to compare the value driver elasticities of EGR and SUS (Equation (A.6)), which are identical by definition, with that of ROIC (Equation (A.4)) to identify the main route for value creation. We obtain the following simplified strategy guidance schema that only requires data on (expected) ROIC and WACC levels (seeAppendix Cfor the derivation): If ROIC is about 2 × WACC (condition II), all three value drivers are important for driving firm value and the three strategies should be prioritized in the shown order. For ROIC levels significantly higher than 2 × WACC (I), firm value increases at the fastest pace when improving EGR and SUS, suggesting an emphasis on brand penetration, followed by geographic expansion. If ROIC is lower (III), raising ROIC promises faster value growth than the route through EGR or SUS, pointing to geographic expansion followed by segmentation as the best strategy emphases to channel CBBE. Finally, firms that are expected to be unprofitable (IV) likely face financial difficulties, in which case implementing cost-cutting and restructuring measures to regain profitability are more advisable than brand leverage measures. We believe that this simple schema of strategy prioritization, derived from our framework, already provides valuable guidance without a detailed analysis of the financial brand leverage strategy. Who Are the Target Decision Makers for Using Our Framework? Our work provides model-driven guidance for strategic brand analysis and planning, including resource allocation. As such, it primarily caters to the needs of C-suite officers, senior marketing and strategy executives, and managers with P&L responsibility. Because our practical implications end with recommendations for a better allocation of resources to implement the most effective market strategies to leverage CBBE, they may be of less relevance to brand and product managers responsible for tactical decisions and marketing mix planning. Given that most decision support models are developed for tactical marketing mix decision variables, we believe our strategic analytics framework is a welcome addition to the literature and offers data-driven decision support for strategy policymakers. What Are the Boundaries of Our Methodology? The strategy guidance schema above has already pointed to a boundary of using our methodology. More generally, our methodology should only be applied to healthy firms that are expected to earn their cost of capital (ROIC > WACC) and generate positive earnings growth (EGR > 0). If expected profitability or earnings growth are negative, however, firm value is expected to deteriorate, which signals that management faces severe problems and should prioritize downsizing, restructuring, and other cost-cutting measures instead of growing and leveraging CBBE. Likewise, our methodology is of limited value for young ventures and startups, which are notoriously difficult to value. The absence of operating history makes estimating growth rates error-prone, survival rates are disproportionally low, and firms are expected to remain unprofitable for a longer period, all of which renders our methodology ineffective. Finally, although we believe in the value of incorporating a capital market perspective into strategic brand management, we view it as an additional aspect and acknowledge that business executives may at times prioritize other objectives that could supersede the firm value maxim. This could for instance occur when executives implement strategies that aim to disrupt markets and industries. Likewise, executives might also choose to invest in CBBE without utilizing its financial leverage at present. Instead, they could treat CBBE as a “reservoir of goodwill” that can be tapped into during times of cost escalation to raise prices and protect profit margins.4 Limitations and Future Research Our study has limitations that may stimulate further research. First, complementary to our focus on the brand, it would be interesting to examine the leveraging role of other market-based assets, such as customer satisfaction or service quality. Second, although the effect of financial value drivers on firm value is exogeneous to brand managers, they might well be influenced by finance managers and their capital market programs. Consequently, the decomposition of the CBBE-firm value effect might be used to identify specific financial value driver elasticities to reinforce, so that the firm can capitalize on the power of the brand to this specific value driver. Third, our findings are based on the Harris Poll EquiTrend metric for measuring CBBE and, strictly speaking, only applicable to this measure. Given the existence of various CBBE metrics, it would be beneficial to replicate our models utilizing alternative measures to broaden the generalizability of our results (Stahl et al. 2012). The authors acknowledge the valuable contributions of Max Backhaus and Tobias Hornig in prior analyses of the data used in this research and thank Kezheng Xu for research assistance in assembling the data set. The authors further thank The Harris Poll for providing access to their EquiTrend data and participants at research seminars and workshops for helpful feedback. Finally, the authors appreciate the insightful comments of Kapil Tuli, Thomas Otter, and Koen Pauwels on an earlier version of the article.

AItitle: 品牌能力缺口达21亿美元：CBBE财务杠杆最优转化框架问世
AIsummary: 该研究聚焦品牌管理领域，探讨企业如何将客户基础品牌资产（CBBE）转化为财务价值这一核心问题。此前文献多关注CBBE的构建与测量，却鲜少量化其财务杠杆潜力，导致企业难以评估品牌价值未被充分开发的程度。针对这一缺口，作者提出一个融合资本市场视角的战略框架，通过分解CBBE对企业价值的传导路径——包括盈利能力（ROIC）、资本成本（WACC）、收益增长率（EGR）和竞争优势持续性（SUS）四大财务驱动因素，并引入品牌细分、市场渗透与地域扩张三大战略调节变量，构建“品牌能力差距”指标。基于2005-2013年598家企业的实证分析显示，企业平均仅实现35%的CBBE财务潜力，潜在价值缺口高达单品牌21亿美元（占企业价值4.3%）。研究创新性地将品牌战略与资本市场需求对齐，为管理层提供优先级矩阵与资源分配模型（如哈雷戴维森案例所示），指导企业通过优化战略重心（如强化渗透策略）激活高价值驱动因子，从而弥合差距。该方法突破了传统品牌评估的静态视角，为动态调整品牌杠杆策略提供了量化工具。
Highlights:
学术要点 1：品牌能力差距（Brand Capabilities Gap） 指企业实际利用客户品牌资产（CBBE）创造的财务价值与其在最优战略下可实现的最大潜在价值之间的差额，通过效率边界分析量化未开发潜力。|||
学术要点 2：战略乘数调节效应（Strategy Emphasis Multipliers） 包含品牌细分、渗透和地理扩展三个维度，通过改变CBBE对财务价值驱动因子（ROIC/EGR/SUS）的边际转化效率，形成差异化的品牌杠杆路径。|||
学术要点 3：财务价值驱动解耦框架（Financial Value Driver Decoupling） 将CBBE对企业价值的影响分解为盈利能力、资本成本、收益增长和超额收益持续性四个异质性传导路径，揭示品牌杠杆效应的结构异质性。|||
学术要点 4：效率边界弹性分析（Efficient Frontier Elasticity） 采用随机前沿模型测算企业将CBBE转化为各财务价值驱动的理论最优弹性，建立动态基准评估品牌战略效率。|||
学术要点 5：战略品牌分析矩阵（Priority Matrix for Strategic Brand Analysis） 通过价值驱动相关性与品牌转化效率的双维度矩阵，定位"错失机会"与"虚假机会"，指导资源向高杠杆领域配置。
